verses.commentary: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit (pleres pneumatos hagiou, πλήρης πνεύματος ἁγίου), returns from the Jordan, led by the Spirit into the wilderness. Lukan emphasis on the Spirit's role at every turning point.
Forty days tempted by the devil (diabolos, διάβολος). Ate nothing — wilderness fast. At the end, hungry. The hunger is the leverage.
Devil: 'If you are the Son of God, tell this stone to become bread.' Same temptation as Matthew 4 — sonship leveraged into self-supply.
Jesus: 'It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone.' Deuteronomy 8:3 — Israel's wilderness lesson. The true Son passes the test Israel failed.
Devil leads him up, shows all the kingdoms of the world in a moment. Spatial-temporal manipulation as temptation backdrop.

'To you I will give all this authority and their glory — it has been delivered to me; I give it to whom I will.' Audacious claim of cosmic dominion, with grain of truth (compare 'ruler of this world,' John 12:31).
'If you, then, will worship me, it will all be yours.' Worship as the price tag. Idolatry is the substitute path to lordship without cross.
Jesus: 'It is written, You shall worship the Lord your God, and him only shall you serve.' Deuteronomy 6:13. The unyielding monotheism.
Devil to Jerusalem, on the pinnacle of the temple. Holy geography weaponized. Lukan order: third temptation in Jerusalem (Luke's narrative magnet — the city of the passion).
Devil cites scripture: Psalm 91:11. Even biblical text deployed for manipulation.
Psalm 91:12 completed: 'They will bear you up, lest you strike your foot.' Promise verses without context become suicide invitations.
Jesus: 'It is said, You shall not put the Lord your God to the test.' Deuteronomy 6:16 — Massah, where Israel tested God. The third Deuteronomy citation in the temptation sequence.
Devil ended every temptation, departed until an opportune time (kairou, καιροῦ). The temptations resume at Gethsemane and the cross. The devil's strategic pause.
Jesus returns to Galilee in the power of the Spirit. Report goes out through all the surrounding country. Pneumatic ministry begins publicly.
Taught in their synagogues, glorified by all. Initial reception positive — the honeymoon phase.
Comes to Nazareth, his hometown. As his custom, enters the synagogue on the Sabbath. Stands to read. Local boy returns as the rabbi.
Given the scroll of Isaiah. Unrolls and finds the place (heuren ton topon, εὗρεν τὸν τόπον — likely selected, not coincidental).

Isaiah 61:1-2 read: 'The Spirit of the Lord is on me — anointed (echrisen, ἔχρισεν, christ-makes) me to proclaim good news to the poor, freedom for captives, recovery of sight for blind, freedom for the oppressed.' Mission statement — fivefold, all aimed downward.
'To proclaim the year of the Lord's favor.' Jubilee (Leviticus 25). Jesus stops mid-quotation, omitting 'day of vengeance' — emphasizes grace over judgment in this inaugural reading.
Rolled up scroll, gave to attendant, sat down (rabbinic teaching posture). Eyes of all in the synagogue fastened (atenizontes, ἀτενίζοντες) on him. Dramatic pause.
'Today (semeron, σήμερον) this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.' Eschatological 'today.' Lukan keyword — the kingdom's now-ness.
All marveled, spoke well of him, at the gracious words. 'Is not this Joseph's son?' Local memory short-circuits Christology. Familiarity breeds dismissal.
Jesus anticipates: 'You will say, Physician, heal yourself; do here in your hometown what we heard happened in Capernaum.' Demands for hometown-specific signs.
'No prophet is acceptable in his hometown.' Restated proverb. Then — confrontation begins.
Many widows in Israel during Elijah's three-and-a-half-year drought. The historical setup.
Yet Elijah sent only to a widow at Zarephath in Sidon (a Gentile woman, 1 Kings 17). Provocative example.
Many lepers in Israel under Elisha — only Naaman the Syrian (a Gentile) was cleansed (2 Kings 5). Two precedents of Gentile inclusion when Israel was bypassed.
All in the synagogue filled with wrath when they heard. The Gentile-inclusion implication infuriates them.
Rise up, drive him out of town, lead him to the brow of the hill to throw him down. Murderous mob from the synagogue. The Messiah rejected on his own ground.
Passing through their midst, he went away. Supernatural escape or imposing presence — either reading possible. The first attempted lynching foreshadows Calvary.
Down to Capernaum, Galilean city. Teaching on the Sabbath. New base of operations.
They were astonished at his teaching, for his word was with authority (exousia, ἐξουσία). The Capernaum response contrasts Nazareth's.
Man in the synagogue with the spirit of an unclean demon. Cries out loudly. Possession even in the synagogue.
'Ha! What have you to do with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God.' Demonic Christology — accurate but exposed.
Jesus rebukes: 'Be silent and come out!' Demon throws the man down, comes out, having done him no harm. Authority recognized.
Amazement: 'What is this word? With authority and power he commands unclean spirits, and they come out.' Word + authority + power — the formula stated by witnesses.
Report spreads to every place in the surrounding region. The news cycle is fast.
Leaves synagogue, enters Simon's house. Simon's mother-in-law sick with high fever. They appeal to Jesus.
Stands over her, rebukes (epetimesen, ἐπετίμησεν — same as demon-rebuke) the fever. It leaves. Immediately she rises and serves them. Disease answers to the same authority as demons.
Sunset — Sabbath ends, bringing of the sick begins. He lays hands on each, heals. Personal touch in volume ministry.
Demons exit, crying, 'You are the Son of God.' He rebukes them, does not allow them to speak, because they knew he was the Christ. The messianic secret operates around demonic confessions.
Daybreak — to a desolate place. Crowds search him out, try to prevent his departure. The popular pressure to settle in one location.
'I must preach the gospel of the kingdom of God to the other cities also; for I was sent for this purpose.' Mission is itinerant; sent (apestalen, ἀπεστάλην) implies the Father's commission.
He kept preaching in the synagogues of Judea (or Galilee — manuscripts vary). Sustained mission across the regions.
